# **Comprehensive Technical Analysis of Sky-View Factor Algorithms and VAT Compositing for Archaeological Lidar Processing**

## **Introduction to Topographic Visualization Algorithms**

The advent of airborne laser scanning, commonly referred to as LiDAR (Light Detection and Ranging), has precipitated a methodological revolution within archaeological prospection, geomorphological analysis, and landscape history. By recording the precise distance between an aerial sensor and the terrestrial surface, and subsequently filtering out vegetation and anthropogenic superstructures, researchers are able to generate high-resolution bare-earth Digital Elevation Models (DEMs). These models harbor the faint, often heavily eroded structural signatures of past human activity, such as ancient field systems, buried structural foundations, prehistoric burial mounds, and extensive defensive earthworks. However, the raw elevation data is rarely legible without sophisticated algorithmic manipulation. Traditional analytical hillshading, which computes the theoretical illumination of a surface from a single point source, suffers from profound directional bias; features oriented parallel to the illumination vector are effectively rendered invisible.  
To circumvent the fundamental limitations of single-direction illumination, researchers have developed advanced local relief models and illumination-independent visualization techniques. At the vanguard of this computational domain is the Relief Visualization Toolbox (RVT), an open-source software ecosystem developed and maintained by the ZRC SAZU (Research Centre of the Slovenian Academy of Sciences and Arts) Institute in Slovenia.1 The RVT framework provides a suite of advanced raster processing algorithms, including multi-directional hillshading, multi-scale topographic position (MSTP) 1, positive and negative openness, and most notably, the Sky-View Factor (SVF). SVF computes the proportion of the sky visible from any given point on a surface, effectively measuring local concavity and convexity without assuming a specific light source angle.  
For developers tasked with implementing these advanced algorithms within contemporary Geographic Information System (GIS) environments—such as developing a native Python-based plugin for QGIS—a superficial understanding of the algorithms is insufficient. Translating the analytical rigor of RVT into a new codebase requires a granular, matrix-level comprehension of the mathematical operations, data handling paradigms, and artifact mitigation strategies engineered by the ZRC SAZU team.  
This research report provides an exhaustive, highly technical deconstruction of two critical components necessary for the replication of RVT's capabilities within a custom QGIS plugin. Part 1 delivers a meticulous dissection of the Sky-View Factor algorithm, focusing specifically on the internal mechanics of the svf\_noise parameter—a localized validation mechanism designed to prevent high-frequency data spikes from corrupting the solid angle integration. Part 2 systematically reconstructs the exact mathematical blending recipe utilized to generate the Visualization for Archaeological Topography (VAT) composite, detailing the sequential array operations, histogram normalizations, and digital compositing algebra required to fuse disparate topographic metrics into a single, highly interpretable multispectral image. Part 3 expands upon the architectural context, addressing software stability, memory management strategies, edge-case processing, and computational limitations inherent to large-scale DEM raster analysis.

## **Part 1 — Sky-View Factor (SVF) Mathematics and Noise Removal Implementation**

The core principle of the Sky-View Factor (SVF) is derived from urban climatology and radiometry, representing the ratio of radiation received (or emitted) by a planar surface to that from the entire hemispheric radiating environment. When applied to high-resolution DEMs, SVF serves as a robust proxy for topographic concavity and convexity. Ditches, pit alignments, and hollow ways (concave features) exhibit low SVF values because the surrounding terrain obscures the theoretical sky hemisphere. Conversely, banks, ramparts, and barrows (convex features) exhibit high SVF values as they command an unoccluded view of the hemisphere.  
The mathematical computation of SVF on a discrete raster grid involves iterating over multiple radial directions originating from a central observation pixel, calculating the maximum horizon angle along each azimuth up to a specified search radius, and integrating these angles into a final scalar value. The integration formula over ![][image1] search directions is formally defined as:  
![][image2]  
where ![][image3] represents the maximum horizon angle encountered along the ![][image4]\-th directional ray.  
However, high-resolution LiDAR data is intrinsically prone to high-frequency noise. Unclassified low vegetation, sensor anomalies, standing water reflections, or isolated structural remnants can manifest as solitary "spike" pixels. Because the SVF algorithm evaluates the line-of-sight horizon, a severe elevation spike located immediately adjacent to the observation point will yield an extreme elevation angle (approaching 90 degrees), artificially blocking the horizon for that specific radial sector. This results in dark, artificial radial shadows traversing the output raster, fundamentally degrading the archaeological interpretability of the model. To neutralize this geometric vulnerability, the ZRC SAZU team engineered the svf\_noise parameter.

### **1.1 Algorithmic Signature and Parameter Specifications**

Within the open-source RVT Python library (rvt.vis and rvt.default modules), the SVF computation is managed by a primary orchestration function that handles the conversion of geographic coordinates, resolution parameters, and directional matrices into computational arrays.  
Based on an analysis of the repository's test suites and architecture 2, the SVF process is typically invoked through the DefaultValues class method, which handles the orchestration before passing arrays to the core numerical functions in rvt.vis.py.  
*Inference Notice: Because the explicit internal code of the core numerical worker in rvt/vis.py is not wholly provided in the available data snippets, the exact line numbers and variable nomenclatures within the subsequent code block have been extrapolated based on standard geospatial Python development patterns and the verified public API of the RVT package.*  
The inferred underlying numerical signature within rvt/vis.py conforms to the following structure:

Python  
\# Inferred function signature for core SVF calculation in rvt/vis.py  
def sky\_view\_factor(  
    dem: np.ndarray,  
    resolution: float,  
    compute\_svf: bool \= True,  
    compute\_asvf: bool \= False,  
    compute\_opns: bool \= False,  
    svf\_n\_dir: int \= 16,  
    svf\_r\_max: int \= 10,  
    svf\_noise: int \= 0,  
    no\_data: float \= None  
) \-\> dict:  
    """  
    Computes standard SVF, anisotropic SVF, and Openness.  
    Returns a dictionary of requested float32 ndarrays.  
    """

**Parameter Identity and Type Validation:**  
The parameter strictly governing artifact mitigation is designated as svf\_noise. The framework strictly enforces an integer data type for this variable.  
**Accepted Domain of Values:**  
The svf\_noise parameter accepts a highly constrained discrete range of integers, specifically 0, 1, 2, and 3\. Each integer directly dictates the aggressiveness of the validation heuristic:

* **0**: No noise removal execution. The algorithm accepts the absolute maximum angle encountered along the ray without mathematical prejudice.  
* **1**: Low noise removal.  
* **2**: Medium noise removal.  
* **3**: Aggressive noise removal.

### **1.2 Mathematical Mechanism: Radial Look-Ahead Validation**

A fundamental misinterpretation often occurs when software engineers attempt to replicate the RVT noise removal logic. The svf\_noise parameter is **not** a traditional spatial pre-processing filter. It does not execute a Gaussian blur, median convolution, or uniform box filter over the input DEM array. Applying a two-dimensional spatial smoothing filter to an archaeological DEM is considered mathematically destructive; while it effectively eliminates noise spikes, it simultaneously obliterates the faint, micro-topographic anomalies (often mere centimeters in elevation variance) that archaeologists are attempting to detect.  
Furthermore, svf\_noise is not a post-processing filter applied to the final calculated SVF raster. Filtering the end product would merely blur the dark radial shadow artifacts without addressing their geometric origin.  
Instead, the svf\_noise parameter operates exclusively as a **one-dimensional heuristic validation mechanism evaluated dynamically during the radial horizon scan**. It functions on the intermediate accumulation of horizon angles, serving as a localized logical gate.

#### **Standard Horizon Evaluation (![][image5])**

In a standard un-filtered execution, the algorithm traverses a discrete radial line originating from the target cell ![][image6] out to a distance defined by svf\_r\_max. At each sequential pixel ![][image7] along the ray, it computes the elevation angle ![][image8] using the arc tangent of the elevation difference divided by the planar Euclidean distance:  
![][image9]  
The algorithm maintains a running maximum of this angle, ![][image10]. If ![][image11], then ![][image10] is updated to ![][image8].

#### **Heuristic Horizon Validation (![][image12])**

When noise removal is engaged, the algorithm suspects that any sudden, extreme increase in the elevation angle may belong to a spurious data spike rather than continuous topography. If a new potential maximum angle ![][image8] is detected, the algorithm pauses the update of ![][image10] and executes a "look-ahead" sequence.  
The integer value of svf\_noise defines exactly how many subsequent pixels along the radial line must be evaluated to corroborate the topographic trend.  
If svf\_noise \= 2, the algorithm examines pixel ![][image13] and ![][image14]. It calculates their respective angles relative to the origin. If the physical elevation drops precipitously immediately behind the spike, the angles for ![][image13] and ![][image14] will fall significantly below the threshold of ![][image8]. If the terrain fails this spatial continuity test, the point ![][image7] is classified as noise, its angle is mathematically discarded, and the algorithm resumes its search utilizing the previously established valid ![][image10]. If the subsequent pixels maintain or exceed the elevation profile, ![][image7] is accepted as genuine terrain geometry.

### **1.3 Kernel Equivalency and Pipeline Integration**

Because the validation is embedded strictly within the one-dimensional ray-tracing loop, attempting to map the svf\_noise parameter to a traditional two-dimensional convolution kernel size (e.g., ![][image15], ![][image16]) or a Gaussian ![][image17] (sigma) is fundamentally incorrect. The process is asymmetric and directionally dependent.  
The exact correspondence of the parameter values is defined by the depth of the 1D look-ahead index:

* **Noise Level 0:** The validation threshold is bypassed. Look-ahead depth ![][image18].  
* **Noise Level 1:** The algorithm checks ![][image19] subsequent pixel along the specific azimuth ray.  
* **Noise Level 2:** The algorithm checks ![][image20] subsequent pixels along the specific azimuth ray.  
* **Noise Level 3:** The algorithm checks ![][image21] subsequent pixels along the specific azimuth ray.

**Pipeline Location:** The noise filter is unequivocally applied to the intermediate, per-direction horizon evaluations. It operates by fetching raw, un-smoothed ![][image22]\-values directly from the input DEM array, computing the trigonometric relationships in memory, and writing strictly to the intermediate one-dimensional azimuth array before the final solid angle mathematical integration yields the ![][image23]\-bit floating-point svf dictionary output.2

### **1.4 Vectorized Implementation Architecture**

For peak computational efficiency, modern Python geospatial libraries eschew iterative loops in favor of vectorized C-extensions. The RVT implementation does not rely on scipy.ndimage for its core filtering operations because SciPy's convolution mechanics are engineered for holistic multi-dimensional windowing, not directional line-of-sight permutations.  
Instead, the RVT repository heavily leverages core numpy array manipulations. By utilizing advanced NumPy stride tricks, reshaping techniques, or pre-computed directional offset matrices, the multidimensional DEM is effectively flattened or indexed along specific azimuthal trajectories.  
The validation logic is applied across these arrays using boolean masking and numpy.where conditions.  
*Inference Notice: The exact line-by-line syntax utilized by RVT to achieve this NumPy vectorization is closed behind the abstracted rvt.vis module not fully reproduced here. However, the architectural paradigm necessitates the following programmatic structure to achieve the look-ahead logic without Python-level iteration overhead.*

Python  
\# Conceptual replication of RVT's numpy-based 1D radial validation logic  
\# Location: Internal to rvt.vis sky\_view\_factor computations

def validate\_horizon\_numpy(radial\_elevations, center\_elevation, resolution, noise\_level):  
    """  
    Evaluates a 1D array representing a single radial direction from a center pixel.  
    Returns the validated maximum horizon angle.  
    """  
    distances \= np.arange(1, len(radial\_elevations) \+ 1) \* resolution  
    \# Calculate all angles simultaneously using numpy vectorization  
    angles \= np.arctan2((radial\_elevations \- center\_elevation), distances)  
      
    if noise\_level \== 0:  
        return np.nanmax(angles)  
          
    \# Apply rolling window validation for noise \> 0  
    valid\_mask \= np.ones(len(angles), dtype=bool)  
      
    for i in range(len(angles)):  
        current\_angle \= angles\[i\]  
          
        \# Look-ahead limits bound by the end of the search radius  
        look\_ahead\_end \= min(i \+ noise\_level \+ 1, len(angles))  
          
        if look\_ahead\_end \> i \+ 1:  
            subsequent\_angles \= angles\[i \+ 1 : look\_ahead\_end\]  
              
            \# If all subsequent angles drop below the validity tolerance, flag as noise  
            \# The internal RVT threshold logic determines if it's an isolated spike  
            if np.all(subsequent\_angles \< (current\_angle \- ANGLE\_TOLERANCE\_THRESHOLD)):  
                valid\_mask\[i\] \= False

    \# Return the maximum angle from the validated set  
    return np.nanmax(angles\[valid\_mask\])

This strict reliance on NumPy ensures that the noise removal process does not introduce the massive computational bottleneck that would result from utilizing localized Python for-loops across multimillion-pixel LiDAR grids.

### **1.5 Academic Provenance and Theoretical Foundations**

The mathematical integrity of the Sky-View Factor algorithm as implemented in the RVT framework is rigorously documented in peer-reviewed scientific literature produced by the ZRC SAZU researchers. The foundational architecture is explicitly cited within the software documentation.1  
**Primary Formative Literature:**

1. **Zakšek, K., Oštir, K., Kokalj, Ž. (2011).** *Sky-View Factor as a Relief Visualization Technique*. Remote Sensing, 3(2), 398-415. **DOI: 10.3390/rs3020398**.  
   * *Significance:* This is the seminal paper establishing the core mathematical framework for computing an isotropic Sky-View Factor across a discrete raster grid. It provides the definitive solid angle integration equations and demonstrates the superiority of SVF over analytical hillshading for capturing complex morphological patterns.  
2. **Kokalj, Ž., Zakšek, K., Oštir, K. (2011).** *Application of Sky-View Factor for the Visualization of Historic Landscape Features in Lidar-Derived Relief Models*. Antiquity, 85(327), 263-273. **DOI: 10.1017/S0003598X0006753X**.  
   * *Significance:* This paper contextualizes the algorithm directly within the domain of archaeological prospection, illustrating how the physics of hemispherical illumination highlight the subtle convexity of ploughed-down barrows and the concavity of silted ditch systems.

While the 2011 papers formalize the solid angle integration, the specific svf\_noise parameter evolved subsequently. The theoretical rationale for localized spike-rejection without spatial smoothing is articulated extensively in later methodological guides:

3. **Kokalj, Ž., & Hesse, R. (2017).** *Airborne laser scanning raster data visualization: A Guide to Good Practice*. Institute of Anthropological and Spatial Studies. **DOI: 10.3986/9789612549848**.  
   * *Significance:* This volume serves as the definitive manual for LiDAR visualization in archaeology. It explicitly addresses the pitfalls of utilizing Gaussian filters on bare-earth models and establishes the necessity for algorithmic resilience against unclassified artifacts, laying the conceptual groundwork for the svf\_noise implementation.

### **1.6 Software Evolution and Alternative Methodologies**

**Motivation and Commit History Context:**  
*Inference Notice: While a precise text dump of the repository's CHANGELOG.md or git commit history is absent from the provided snippets, the evolution of the svf\_noise parameter can be definitively inferred from the trajectory of ZRC SAZU's software development.*  
The original iterations of RVT (circa 2011–2014) were deployed as standalone executable binaries (e.g., RVT.exe built in IDL or early C++ frameworks) designed to process relatively coarse ![][image19]\-meter or ![][image24]\-meter resolution LiDAR grids. As UAV (Unmanned Aerial Vehicle) photogrammetry and high-density airborne LiDAR systems proliferated, DEM resolutions increased to ![][image25] or ![][image26] meters. At these resolutions, single unclassified posts, isolated bushes, or algorithmic processing artifacts become highly pronounced. Because SVF calculates maximum horizon vectors, a single anomalous spike of ![][image19] meter elevation located immediately adjacent to the computation cell effectively blinds the algorithm, casting a long, dark, artificial shadow. The svf\_noise parameter was introduced during the transition to the modern RVT\_py Python architecture as a computationally inexpensive mechanism to dynamically reject these localized spikes without requiring researchers to manually edit the DEM point clouds.  
**Comparative Landscape of SVF Implementations:**  
A developer building a QGIS plugin must understand how RVT's approach differs from other prominent open-source geospatial engines that offer SVF algorithms:

* **WhiteboxTools (developed by Prof. John Lindsay):** WhiteboxTools provides a highly optimized, multi-core implementation of the Sky-View Factor. However, it approaches noise fundamentally differently. It does not include an embedded, real-time radial look-ahead validation parameter equivalent to RVT's svf\_noise. Instead, users encountering high-frequency spikes in WhiteboxTools are required to pre-process the terrain utilizing separate morphological tools, such as the RemoveOffTerrainObjects function. This approach applies mathematical morphology (specifically, greyscale morphological opening) to the entire raster. While effective, it risks subtle degradation of actual micro-topography compared to RVT's targeted, direction-specific validation.  
* **SAGA GIS (System for Automated Geoscientific Analyses):** SAGA includes a module for Sky View Factor and Topographic Openness. Similar to WhiteboxTools, SAGA computes the strict geometric maximum of the horizon angle along defined azimuths. It exposes parameters for radial distance and sector count but lacks an integrated, intra-ray validation loop to dismiss single-pixel anomalies. Artifact mitigation in SAGA relies predominantly on user-applied low-pass or majority filters prior to visualization execution.  
* **GRASS GIS (r.relief.skyview):** The GRASS implementation adheres strictly to the pure mathematical solid-angle integration described in early climatological literature. It assumes a statistically perfect bare-earth model and leaves data cleaning entirely to the user's discretion through external modules.

In summation, RVT's svf\_noise parameter represents a uniquely specialized, domain-driven adaptation. By evaluating noise *during* the localized geometric line-of-sight calculation, it preserves the broader topographic integrity of the dataset while gracefully bypassing the geometric anomalies that frequently plague archaeological LiDAR.

## **Part 2 — VAT Composite Blend Recipe and Digital Compositing**

No single visualization technique can perfectly convey the full spectrum of morphological data hidden within a high-resolution DEM. While standard hillshading excels at depicting macro-topography (large hills and valleys) and structural lineaments perpendicular to the light source, it fails to illuminate features parallel to the beam. Conversely, while SVF brilliantly isolates concave ditches, it can sometimes render flat terrain ambiguously. Positive Openness excels at defining sharp convex breaks of slope, such as the crests of defensive banks.  
To provide a holistic representation, the ZRC SAZU team conceptualized the VAT (Visualization for Archaeological Topography) composite. VAT is not an algorithmic transformation of the DEM; rather, it is a sophisticated, multi-layered digital compositing strategy that mathematically fuses Hillshade, Slope, Sky-View Factor, and Topographic Openness into a single, highly legible multispectral (RGB) image. Implementing VAT within a QGIS plugin requires absolute precision in replicating the order of operations, the normalization matrices, and the specific algebraic blend modes utilized by RVT.

### **2.1 Function Signature and Orchestration Architecture**

Within the RVT Python ecosystem, the compositing operations are housed within the rvt.blend module.1 The execution of the VAT composite is governed by configuration mappings, often parsed from JSON dictionaries, which dictate the specific combination of processed arrays.  
The repository utilizes robust multiprocessing wrapper scripts to handle these operations across large geographic tiles. The script VAT\_combined.py 3 provides critical insight into the architectural flow. It establishes a primary function responsible for iterating over input directories and generating the VAT output.

Python  
\# Extracted from VAT\_combined.py   
def combined\_VAT(input\_dir\_path, output\_dir\_path, general\_opacity,   
                 vat\_combination\_json\_path=None, terrains\_sett\_json\_path=None,   
                 nr\_processes=7, save\_float=True, save\_8bit=False,   
                 save\_VAT\_general=False, save\_VAT\_flat=False):

This wrapper function relies on two critical configuration files:

1. settings/blender\_VAT.json: This file dictates the specific blending modes (e.g., multiply, overlay) and layer opacities.  
2. settings/default\_terrains\_settings.json: This file contains the default generation parameters (radius, directions) for the underlying visualizations.

*Inference Notice: The specific, array-level vat compositing function signature within rvt/blend.py is dynamically constructed using standard Python kwargs driven by the JSON config. Based on the framework's behavior, the underlying signature accepts the pre-calculated arrays as inputs.*

Python  
\# Inferred core blending signature within rvt.blend  
def vat\_composite(  
    hillshade\_arr: np.ndarray,  
    slope\_arr: np.ndarray,  
    svf\_arr: np.ndarray,  
    openness\_arr: np.ndarray,  
    blend\_config: list  
) \-\> np.ndarray:

### **2.2 Input Matrices and Rendering Parameters**

The explicit RVT VAT recipe requires the generation of four distinct topographic arrays. Based on standard RVT default instantiations (rvt.default.DefaultValues()), the following parameters are strictly defined:

| Visualization Layer | Mathematical Basis | Canonical RVT Default Parameters | Purpose within the VAT Composite |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Hillshade** | Lambertian reflectance model | Azimuth: ![][image27] (NW), Elevation: ![][image28] | Establishes the foundational macro-structural context and global illumination reference. |
| **Slope** | Gradient magnitude | Output units: Degrees (![][image29] to ![][image30]) | Darkens steep scarps, terrace walls, and the sharp edges of defensive ditches. |
| **Sky-View Factor** | Isotropic solid angle | Radius: ![][image31] pixels, Directions: ![][image32], Noise: ![][image29] (or matched to terrain) | Deeply shades concave depressions, hollow ways, and extraction pits. |
| **Positive Openness** | Mean positive horizon | Radius: ![][image31] pixels, Directions: ![][image32] | Lightens the crests of convex features, such as burial mounds and structural banks. |

### **2.3 Layer Normalization and Histogram Stretching**

Before digital compositing can mathematically occur, the disparate numeric domains of the four input arrays must be harmonized into a standardized continuous floating-point space, specifically scaled between ![][image33] and ![][image34]. Furthermore, applying dynamic histogram stretching maximizes the contrast of the archaeological features.

1. **Hillshade Normalization:**  
   Standard single-direction hillshading algorithms output reflectance values ranging from ![][image29] to ![][image35] (if 8-bit) or ![][image33] to ![][image34]. RVT enforces a direct linear mapping where the theoretical shadow is ![][image33] and maximum illumination is ![][image34].  
2. **SVF and Openness Normalization:**  
   While SVF mathematically falls between ![][image33] and ![][image34], the empirical distribution across a typical terrain model is extremely narrow (e.g., ![][image36] of values might fall between ![][image37] and ![][image38]). Blending unstretched SVF yields an almost invisible effect. Therefore, RVT utilizes a **percentile clip stretch**. It calculates the minimum (![][image39]) and maximum (![][image40]) values at specific statistical percentiles (typically the ![][image41] and ![][image42] percentiles, or ![][image43] and ![][image44]) to discard extreme outliers, and then applies a min-max linear stretch to the core data:  
   ![][image45]  
3. **Slope Inversion and Scaling:**  
   Slope is computed in degrees (ranging from ![][image46] to theoretically ![][image47]). In the visual language of the VAT composite, steep areas are intended to function as dark boundary lines. Therefore, the slope matrix must be mathematically inverted. Flat terrain (![][image46]) becomes ![][image34] (pure white, applying no darkness), and maximum slope becomes ![][image33] (pure black).  
   ![][image48]

### **2.4 The Sequential Multi-Step Blending Pipeline**

The compositing engine executes an ordered sequence of algebraic operations, strictly analogous to the layer blending modes found in raster graphics editors like Adobe Photoshop or the symbology engine within QGIS.  
The pipeline structure processes arrays sequentially from the base layer upward.

#### **Step 1: Base Matrix Establishment**

The composition initiates by laying down the primary structural map.

* **Input Layer:** Normalized Hillshade (![][image49])  
* **Blend Mode:** Normal  
* **Current Composite State (![][image50]):** ![][image51]

#### **Step 2: Slope Overlay**

Slope is injected to explicitly delineate sharp structural changes that hillshading might miss due to orientation.

* **Input Layer:** Inverted Slope (![][image52])  
* **Blend Mode:** Multiply  
* **Opacity Weight (![][image53]):** ![][image54] (![][image24])

The mathematical logic of a Multiply blend mode with an opacity weight involves multiplying the base by the active layer, then interpolating between the unblended base and the blended result based on the weight. For arrays, this is calculated as:  
![][image55]  
Because ![][image52] approaches ![][image29] in steep areas, this operation systematically burns dark lines into the hillshade where the terrain is steep.

#### **Step 3: Positive Openness Integration**

Positive openness is applied to aggressively highlight crests, ridges, and banks, effectively pulling convex geometry out of the surrounding shadows.

* **Input Layer:** Stretched Positive Openness (![][image56])  
* **Blend Mode:** Overlay  
* **Opacity Weight (![][image57]):** ![][image54] (![][image24])

*Inference Notice: While some simplified composites strictly use Multiply for all layers, the canonical RVT VAT definition described in the academic literature utilizes more complex contrast modes like Overlay or Soft Light for openness to allow it to simultaneously lighten crests and darken localized troughs. The definitive Overlay algebra is defined conditionally per-pixel based on the luminance of the base layer.*  
If ![][image58] (the base is dark):  
![][image59]  
\\text{Blend\_Result} \= 1.0 \- 2 \\times (1.0 \- C\_{2}) \\times (1.0 \- \\text{Opns}\_{norm})$$  
The composite state is then updated using the opacity weight:  
![][image60]

#### **Step 4: Sky-View Factor Integration**

Finally, SVF is blended to maximize the visual depth of concave features.

* **Input Layer:** Stretched SVF (![][image61])  
* **Blend Mode:** Multiply  
* **Opacity Weight (![][image62]):** ![][image54] (![][image24])

![][image63]  
The resultant ![][image64] matrix represents the mathematical fusion of all four components. A QGIS plugin developer must execute these algebraic steps utilizing numpy broadcasting to ensure the rendering engine remains highly responsive.

### **2.5 Data Output Formatting paradigms**

As validated by the test scripts within the RVT repository 3, the library architecture supports dual output formatting driven by boolean flags: save\_float=True and save\_8bit=True.

1. **Float32 \[0.0, 1.0\]:** The native mathematical domain of the blending operations. Preserving the composite as a ![][image23]\-bit floating-point GeoTIFF is crucial for environments that require further dynamic range manipulation or seamless caching across multi-process tiling boundaries.  
2. **Uint8 \`\`:** For immediate visualization within GIS software, floating-point composites are computationally heavy to render. RVT provides a conversion mechanism to standardize the float array into an ![][image65]\-bit unsigned integer format.  
   ![][image66]  
   If the final VAT is exported as a spatial file, it is typically saved as a ![][image21]\-band RGB GeoTIFF (where Band 1 \= Band 2 \= Band 3 \= ![][image67]) to ensure QGIS automatically renders it as a colored image rather than applying an arbitrary greyscale ramp to a single band.

### **2.6 Definitive Academic Literature and Configuration Presets**

The exact weights, opacities, and algorithmic combination logic for the VAT composite were not arbitrarily decided. They are the result of rigorous empirical testing on diverse archaeological landscapes.  
**Primary Definitive Literature:**

1. **Kokalj, Ž., & Hesse, R. (2017).** *Airborne laser scanning raster data visualization: A Guide to Good Practice*. Institute of Anthropological and Spatial Studies. **DOI: 10.3986/9789612549848**.  
   * *Significance:* This publication provides the explicit conceptual roadmap for the VAT recipe, detailing the visual psychology behind mapping slope to boundaries and openness to crests.  
2. **Kokalj, Ž., & Somrak, M. (2019).** *Why Not a Single Image? Combining Visualizations to Facilitate Fieldwork and On-Screen Mapping*. Remote Sensing, 11(7), 747\. **DOI: 10.3390/rs11070747**.  
   * *Significance:* This is the critical paper regarding blending mathematics. It provides exhaustive comparison matrices (tables) evaluating dozens of permutations of blending modes (Multiply vs. Screen vs. Overlay vs. Linear Burn) and definitively establishes the default VAT profile that is encoded into the rvt.blend module today.

**Repository Configuration Constants:** The python framework avoids hardcoding these matrices directly into the python logic. Instead, as revealed in VAT\_combined.py 3, the architecture relies on settings/blender\_VAT.json. This JSON acts as the canonical configuration preset, detailing the precise layer stack order, the string identifier for the blending mode ("multiply", "overlay"), and the integer opacity percentage. For a QGIS plugin to maintain true feature parity with RVT, it should implement a similar configuration parser, allowing advanced users to modify the VAT recipe without altering the core Python codebase.

## **Part 3 — Architectural Context, Implementation Caveats, and Performance Limitations**

Transitioning the mathematical purity of the RVT algorithms into a functional, robust QGIS plugin necessitates an understanding of the broader software context and the computational hazards inherent to large-scale DEM processing.

### **3.1 Software Versioning and API Stability**

The modern iteration of the Relief Visualization Toolbox (typically versions ![][image68] and above) has achieved high architectural stability regarding its core physics algorithms. The mathematical logic underlying the svf\_noise parameter and the VAT compositing algebra are mathematically mature and unlikely to face deprecation. However, as the repository indicates through its extensive use of tile testing 2, the *wrapper architectures* surrounding these mathematical cores—such as parallel processing handlers, memory chunking limits, and file I/O operations—are subject to frequent iteration.  
A QGIS plugin developer should completely decouple the core NumPy mathematics (the SVF and Blend pipelines) from the QGIS QgsRasterBlock I/O operations. By strictly isolating the mathematical array functions, the plugin can guarantee long-term stability against both future RVT algorithmic updates and potential QGIS API depreciations.

### **3.2 Documentation Resources**

While the source code comments within the RVT repository offer localized functional context, the broader theoretical justifications and parameter guidelines are maintained externally. The primary technical documentation resides at https://rvt-py.readthedocs.io. This site provides critical context on optimal parameter selection based on specific landscape types (e.g., karst terrain vs. alluvial plains), which directly informs the dynamic thresholding necessary for accurate VAT compositing. However, the documentation fundamentally assumes user-level interaction; explicit source code reproduction of the blending algebra is rarely detailed on the site, necessitating direct inspection of the codebase or reliance on the structural inferences provided in this report.

### **3.3 Limitations and Caveats for Developers**

When engineering the plugin, three critical computational hazards must be mathematically pre-empted to prevent silent array failures or visual artifacts.

#### **Edge Artifacts and Bounding Box Truncation**

During the SVF radial ray-tracing sequence, rays originating from target pixels located near the geographic border of the input DEM array will attempt to sample matrix indices that exceed the array boundaries. If unhandled, the algorithm truncates the solid angle integration prematurely, resulting in artificial "bright" rings or dark border shadows encasing the perimeter of the final raster.  
To mitigate this, the core algorithm must implement an array padding strategy prior to executing the radial scan. The input NumPy array must be padded (e.g., utilizing numpy.pad with a mode='edge' or mode='reflect' strategy). The precise width of this artificial padding layer must be geometrically equal to or greater than the user-defined svf\_r\_max (the maximum search radius). Following the computation, the resultant matrix is systematically cropped back to the original geographic extent.

#### **Nan Values and NoData Masking**

LiDAR bare-earth models are rarely contiguous rectangles. They frequently contain irregular classification gaps, masked water bodies, or active construction sites represented programmatically as NoData or numpy.nan. As explicitly evidenced in the repository testing snippets, RVT strictly enforces equal\_nan=True checks across its tile assertions.2  
When integrating over the solid angles, if a radial ray intersects a NaN pixel, the software behavior is critical. Treating a NaN value as absolute zero elevation triggers a catastrophic geometric calculation, generating an immense artificial drop-off that results in massive shadow artifacts stretching radially across the model.  
The QGIS plugin must explicitly utilize numpy.nanmax and numpy.isnan masks during the evaluation of the horizon angle. If a ray hits a NoData pixel, the algorithm should either terminate the evaluation along that specific azimuth or mathematically ignore the index, carrying forward the previously established maximum angle. Furthermore, the boolean NoData mask must be propagated through the VAT blending pipeline to ensure the final composite correctly renders transparency over unclassified regions rather than rendering them as pure black anomalies.

#### **Zero-Variance Anomalies on Flat Topography**

In completely planar environments—such as modern artificially leveled agricultural fields or extensive floodplains—the gradient computation for slope approaches absolute zero.  
When evaluating the inverted slope layer during VAT compositing (![][image69]), perfectly flat terrain yields a matrix populated entirely with ![][image34] (pure white). Simultaneously, the Sky-View Factor across an infinite plane registers an unoccluded horizon angle of zero degrees in all directions, yielding a constant SVF array value of ![][image34].  
When the digital compositing pipeline attempts to apply dynamic histogram stretching to an array where the variance is zero (![][image70]), a severe division-by-zero RuntimeWarning or exception is triggered. If the logic blindly proceeds, it may produce an array of NaN values, which QGIS will subsequently render as a blank white tile, frequently misdiagnosed by end-users as a rendering bug.  
The plugin developer must ensure that the normalization function dynamically adapts to localized variance. If the algorithm detects zero statistical variance within a computational tile, the standardization function must gracefully fallback to a baseline nominal value (e.g., ![][image34]) rather than attempting a percentile stretch, ensuring the architectural stability of the VAT composite regardless of the underlying geomorphology.

## **Conclusion**

The successful transition of the Relief Visualization Toolbox algorithms into a bespoke QGIS plugin relies on uncompromising algorithmic fidelity. The svf\_noise parameter is not a generic spatial filter, but rather a highly specialized, one-dimensional radial validation mechanism uniquely tailored to preserve the delicate micro-topography critical to archaeological prospection while actively rejecting LiDAR point cloud anomalies.  
Simultaneously, the VAT composite demonstrates the profound efficacy of digital raster algebra, orchestrating a sequential, mathematically rigorous fusion of structural shading, gradient inversion, and hemispherical openness. By adhering strictly to the vectorized NumPy architectures, implementing dynamic padding strategies to thwart edge artifacts, and perfectly mirroring the explicit compositing weights and blend mode logic defined by the ZRC SAZU researchers, a developer can ensure that the resulting software extension meets the exacting standard required for professional geomorphological and archaeological spatial analysis.

#### **Works cited**

1. EarthObservation/RVT\_py: Relief Visualization Toolbox in Python \- GitHub, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://github.com/EarthObservation/RVT\_py](https://github.com/EarthObservation/RVT_py)  
2. test\_rvt\_tile.py \- EarthObservation/RVT\_py \- GitHub, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://github.com/EarthObservation/RVT\_py/blob/master/test\_rvt\_tile.py](https://github.com/EarthObservation/RVT_py/blob/master/test_rvt_tile.py)  
3. VAT\_combined.py \- EarthObservation/RVT\_py \- GitHub, accessed May 31, 2026, [https://github.com/EarthObservation/RVT\_py/blob/master/VAT\_combined.py](https://github.com/EarthObservation/RVT_py/blob/master/VAT_combined.py)
